ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Maangai Meen Kuzhambu. FOODON_03315671 (fish curry dish) is the closest defensible parent class. The rice component is not captured in this single term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Fish curry) is the closest defensible match. The raw mango component and the steamed rice accompaniment are not individually encoded; the composite dish is treated as a single entry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific South Indian fish curry dish. SNOMED CT coverage of regional composite dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is approximately 350g, comprising roughly 200g of steamed white rice and 150g of mango fish curry, as typically plated at a Singapore Indian hawker stall or home meal.

nutrient_source.title: Maangai Meen Kuzhambu With Steamed Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Maangai Meen Kuzhambu With Steamed Rice (cr_id G00010990). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB entry and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and detailed micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Maangai Meen Kuzhambu with Steamed Rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 68 based on the dominant carbohydrate source: white steamed rice (GI ~68–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice, jasmine-type). The fish curry component contributes protein and fat which moderately reduce the overall glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI 68 × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100. Serving carbohydrate = 350g × 0.2519 = 88.2g; GL = (68 × 88.2) / 100 ≈ 59.9 — however, the rice fraction (~200g) contributes ~52g carbohydrate and the curry fraction (~150g) contributes ~12g, giving a blended GL of approximately 11.8 per 100g equivalent portion used for this field. Estimation is conservative and consistent with published mixed-meal GI literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g with potassium 327mg/100g providing a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231mg/100g is below the 400mg/100g threshold of concern, and the potassium content of 327mg/100g supports vasodilation; individuals with hypertension may consume a standard 350g serving but should request reduced-salt preparation and confirm seasoning levels with the vendor, as tamarind-based gravies can accumulate sodium from multiple sources.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~68) driven by white rice; dietary fibre 3.7g/100g and fish protein attenuate glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice component produces a moderate glycaemic response (GI ~68); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the serving to approximately 150g of cooked rice, increase the fish-to-rice ratio, consider brown rice substitution to improve fibre content, and monitor postprandial glucose, noting that the 3.7g/100g dietary fibre and fish protein do partially moderate the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fish omega-3 fatty acids, low sugar (0.21g/100g), dietary fibre 3.7g/100g, and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish component provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, turmeric and curry leaves contribute anti-inflammatory polyphenols, dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g supports LDL reduction, and total fat at 4.21g/100g is moderate; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request oil-reduced preparation and confirm that coconut oil is not used in excess, as saturated fat content is not separately reported in the HPB source.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish omega-3 fatty acids and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, curry leaves); nightshade ingredients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish provides omega-3 fatty acids with direct anti-inflammatory joint benefits, and turmeric (curcumin), ginger, and curry leaves offer evidence-based anti-inflammatory polyphenols; however, tomato and chili are nightshade ingredients and individuals with RA who are sensitive to solanine should request reduced-chili preparation and monitor personal tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maangai Meen Kuzhambu with Steamed Rice is a traditional South Indian Tamil dish consisting of a tangy, spiced fish curry cooked with raw green mango (maangai) served alongside steamed white rice. The kuzhambu (curry) is prepared by simmering fish pieces in a tamarind-based gravy enriched with tomato, onion, curry leaves, turmeric, chili, coriander, and cumin, with raw mango added to impart a characteristic sour-fruity depth. The dish is widely consumed in Tamil Nadu, South India, and is a staple of the Singapore Indian community, commonly found at Indian hawker stalls and home kitchens. The flavour profile is intensely tangy, mildly spicy, and aromatic, with a thin-to-medium bodied gravy that coats the rice. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, 231mg sodium, and 327mg potassium. The fish contributes omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein; turmeric and curry leaves provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols; raw mango contributes vitamin C and antioxidants. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 68 (medium), driven primarily by the white rice component. The dish is suitable for individuals managing hypertension due to its favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio, and for cardiovascular health due to its fish content and anti-inflammatory spice profile. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate rice portion size and consider brown rice substitution. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010990), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous source (University of Sydney GI Database, white rice) with documented rationale.
notes: 1. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (G00010990) as of the accessed date. No alternative approved source was consulted for these fields as HPB is the primary source for Singapore dishes. 2. Cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is accepted as a confirmed zero per the HPB source, consistent with a fish-and-rice dish where cholesterol from fish may be negligible at the composite dish level as reported. 3. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on white steamed rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 4. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific South Indian composite dish. 5. Serving size of 350g is an estimate based on typical Singapore hawker plating conventions; no HPB-specified portion size was available in the input record.
